verbatim_quote: He dreamt an old dream, of three knights in white cloaks, and a tower long fallen, and Lyanna in her bed of blood.
note: Exact match in Ned's dream of Tower of Joy.

verbatim_quote: Nor will I. Leave it be, Robert, for the love you say you bear me. I dishonored myself and I dishonored Catelyn, in the sight of gods and men.
note: Exact match - Ned explicitly acknowledges dishonoring both himself and Catelyn.

verbatim_quote: Robert would swear undying love and forget them before evenfall, but Ned Stark kept his vows.
note: Ned's reputation for keeping vows established explicitly.
Extensively searched - 'blood' phrase too common; no clear single passage where Ned calls Jon his 'blood' but not son. May be interpretive summary rather than direct quote.

verbatim_quote: She even looked like Jon, with the long face and brown hair of the Starks, and nothing of their lady mother in her face or her coloring.
note: Sansa describes Arya looking like Jon and Stark line.

verbatim_quote: He had lived his lies for fourteen years, yet they still haunted him at night.
note: Lie about Jon (age 14) haunts Ned despite determination to keep the secret.

verbatim_quote: Your Grace, the girl is scarcely more than a child. You are no Tywin Lannister, to slaughter innocents.
note: Ned's explicit refusal of Robert's order to murder Daenerys; part of pattern protecting Targaryen children.

verbatim_quote: Ned had named that murder; Robert called it war. When he had protested that the young prince and princess were no more than babes, his new-made king had replied, "I see no babes. Only dragonspawn."
note: Ned opposed the Sack murders; shows pattern of protecting Targaryen heirs.

Tower of Joy scene mentions three Kingsguard (Dayne, Whent, Hightower) but books never explain their presence there or name them guarding an heir. This is theory-interpretation based on circumstance, not explicit text.

verbatim_quote: A blue flower grew from a chink in a wall of ice, and filled the air with sweetness.
note: Exact match from Daenerys's House of Undying vision.

verbatim_quote: He could see it still: a crown of winter roses, blue as frost.
note: Harrenhal tourney memory; also agot-eddard-13.md confirms 'pale blue roses' imagery with Lyanna.

verbatim_quote: Prince Rhaegar Targaryen urged his horse past his own wife, the Dornish princess Elia Martell, to lay the queen of beauty's laurel in Lyanna's lap. He could see it still: a crown of winter roses, blue as frost.
note: Explicit description of Rhaegar choosing Lyanna over Elia at Harrenhal tourney.

verbatim_quote: A storm of rose petals blew across a blood-streaked sky, as blue as the eyes of death.
note: Tower of Joy dream combines rose imagery with blue symbolism and Lyanna.

verbatim_quote: She wore a garland of pale blue roses, and her eyes wept blood.
note: Lyanna's statue in Winterfell crypts described in Ned's dream.
Theory-interpretation connecting separate textual elements (blue flower + wall of ice + Jon at Wall). No explicit text links them this way; vision's meaning is unresolved in canon.
Entirely theory-driven. 'Song of Ice and Fire' is series title/prophecy concept, not explicitly applied to any character in text.
Theory-synthesis of separate abilities (Stark/warging, Targaryen/dragons). No text connects them for Jon.
Entirely speculative. No text applies Prince That Was Promised or Azor Ahai titles to Jon; prophecies remain enigmatic.
Hypothetical legal interpretation not in text. Succession rules would determine actual throne claims.
Based on historical Targaryen precedent (Aegon with two wives) but no text confirms Rhaegar/Lyanna could or did marry.
Theory discussion of legitimization precedent (Robert legitimized bastards) not directly applied to Jon in text.

verbatim_quote: I shall take no wife, hold no lands, father no children. I shall wear no crowns and win no glory. I shall live and die at my post.
note: Exact match from binding Night's Watch oath creating textual obstacle to any throne claim.
Plausible argument but not stated in text. No canon discussion of evidence Jon could marshal for a claim.
Speculative about Jon's character motivations; no text explores this directly.

verbatim_quote: She even looked like Jon, with the long face and brown hair of the Starks, and nothing of their lady mother in her face or her coloring.
note: Thematic support: Jon's bastard alienation and mother-unknown are core character elements throughout AGOT.
